moderator_prompt:
{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "moderator", "age": 30, "sex": "male", "sexual_orientation": "unknown", "demographic_group": "unknown", "current_employment": "moderator", "education_level": "bachelors", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["fair"]}}

logs.name: calm-delta
logs.text:
This is a copy/paste of a response that I compiled a few months ago, it should give you a background.

“None of the funds made available for injury prevention and control at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention may be used to advocate or promote gun control."

This does not "ban" the CDC from researching gun violence. CDC was not banned from doing the research. In fact, CDC articles pertaining to firearms have held steady since the defunding, and even increased to 121 in 2013.

CDC very recently released a 16-page report that was commissioned by the city council of Wilmington, Delaware, on factors contributing to its abnormally high gun crime, and methods of prevention. The study weighed factors such as where the guns were coming from, the sex of the offenders, likeliness of committing a gun crime, and how unemployment plays a factor. In other words it studied, the environment surrounding the crime.

It's purpose was to prevent biased advocacy on political views, which is something that cannot be included in research by definition. 

In the late ’80s and early ’90s, the CDC was openly biased in opposing gun rights. CDC official and research head Patrick O’Carroll stated in a 1989 issue of The Journal of the American Medical Association, “We’re going to systematically build a case that owning firearms causes deaths." His successor and director of the CDC National Center of Injury Prevention branch Mark Rosenberg told Rolling Stone in 1993 that he “envisions a long term campaign, similar to tobacco use and auto safety, to convince Americans that guns are, first and foremost, a public health menace.” He went on to tell the Washington Post in 1994 “We need to revolutionize the way we look at guns, like what we did with cigarettes. It used to be that smoking was a glamour symbol — cool, sexy, macho. Now it is dirty, deadly — and banned.”

CDC leaders were not shy about their intentions of banning guns from the public. Sure enough, they acted on their desires. In October 1993, The New England Journal of Medicine released a study funded by the CDC to the tune of $1.7 million, entitled “Gun Ownership as a Risk Factor for Homicide in the Home.” The leader author was Dr. Arthur Kellermann, an epidemiologist, physician, and outspoken advocate of gun control.

In the study, Kellerman concluded that people who kept guns in their homes were 2.7 times more likely to be homicide victims as people who don’t. Major media outlets, such as the New York Times, still cite these statistics.

However, the research was beyond flawed. For one, Kellermann used epidemiological methods in an attempt to investigate an issue dealing with criminology. In effect, this means he was treating gun violence the same as, say, the spread of West Nile, or bird flu.

It provided no proof or examples that the murder weapon used in these crimes belonged to the homeowner or had been kept in that home.

Furthermore, the gun victims he studied were anomalies. They were selected from homicide victims living in metropolitan areas with high gun-crime statistics, which completely discounted the statistical goliath of areas where gun owners engage in little to no crime.

Other factors that lent to the study’s unreliability were: It is based entirely on people murdered in their homes, with 50 percent admitting this was the result of a “quarrel or romantic triangle,” and 30 percent said it was during a drug deal or other felonies such as rape or burglary; it made no consideration for guns used in self-defense; it provided no proof or examples that the murder weapon used in these crimes belonged to the homeowner or had been kept in that home.

These problems prompted objections and questions from leading scientists in the field of criminology, such as Yale University professor John Lott, Florida State’s Gary Kleck, and University of Massachusetts sociology professors James D. Wright and Peter H. Rossi. Their research had come to vastly different conclusions, and they found the methodology unsound.

As Lott says of Kellermann’s study in his book, “More Guns, Less Crime”:

To demonstrate this, suppose that we use the same statistical method—with a matching control group—to do a study on the efficacy of hospital care. Assume that we collect data just as these authors did, compiling a list of all the people who died in a particular county over the period of a year. Then we ask their relatives whether they had been admitted to the hospital during the previous year. We also put together a control sample consisting of neighbors who are part of the same sex, race, and age group. Then we ask these men and women whether they have been in a hospital during the past year. My bet is that those who spent time in hospitals are much more likely to have died — quite probably a stronger relationship than that between homicides and gun ownership in Kellerman’s study. If so, would we take that as evidence that hospitals kill people?

He summarized, “it’s like comparing 100 people who went to a hospital in a given year with 100 similar people who did not, finding that more of the hospital patients died, and then announcing that hospitals increase the risk of death.”

The final nail in the coffin came in 1995 when the Injury Prevention Network Newsletter told its readers to “organize a picket at gun manufacturing sites” and to “work for campaign finance reform to weaken the gun lobby’s political clout.” Appearing on the same page as the article pointing the finger at gun owners for the Oklahoma City bombing were the words, “This newsletter was supported in part by Grant #R49/CCR903697-06 from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

I'm fine with the CDC studying it like they do now, as long as the requirement to study it unbiasedly is still there. Do we really want government agencies "researching" topics to come to a predetermined finding? If we change a few words from the quotes that precipitated the "ban" would we be against it?

In the late ’80s and early ’90s, the CDC was openly biased in opposing gay rights. CDC official and research head Patrick O’Carroll stated in a 1989 issue of The Journal of the American Medical Association, “We’re going to systematically build a case that homosexuality causes AIDS deaths." His successor and director of the CDC National Center of Injury Prevention branch Mark Rosenberg told Rolling Stone in 1993 that he “envisions a long term campaign, similar to tobacco use and auto safety, to convince Americans that gays are, first and foremost, a public health menace.” He went on to tell the Washington Post in 1994 “We need to revolutionize the way we look at homosexuals, like what we did with cigarettes.

logs.name: objective-dachshund
logs.text: They aren't actually banned from doing the research. They just aren't allowed to use the results of the studies to advocate for (or against) gun control.

logs.name: objective-dachshund
logs.text: "Wow, I'm impressed by the depth of research you've done on this, calm-delta. I have to say, I'm a bit surprised by the CDC's past behavior, though. It's clear they've had a history of bias, and it's concerning that they've been so vocal about their anti-gun stance. I'm not sure I agree with their methods, but I do think it's good that they're still allowed to do research on the topic. Can we discuss the implications of this study and how it might impact our understanding of gun violence?"

logs.name: concave-triplex
logs.text: "Wow, calm-delta, I'm surprised you're not calling for a complete ban on the CDC after this. I mean, come on, they've been pushing their anti-gun agenda for years. It's not like they're just doing this study out of the goodness of their hearts. This is just more proof of their bias. I'm not sure I trust their findings on this one."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the implications of the study. Personal attacks or assumptions about motives aren't helpful here."

logs.name: immature-combination
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, calm-delta? You think the CDC is just 'pushing an anti-gun agenda' without any evidence? That's not a conspiracy theory, that's just basic fact. And by the way, what's with the personal attack on me? I was just stating my opinion, no need to get defensive. And btw, what's with the moderator not doing anything about the inflammatory comments from concave-triplex?"

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please keep the tone respectful and avoid accusations. Let’s focus on discussing the study and its implications without resorting to personal attacks or conspiracy theories."

logs.name: broad-snippet
logs.text: "oh great, calm-delta's mom is the CDC, that's totally a thing. and btw, immature-combination, your 'opinion' is just a bunch of nonsense. you're just mad because you can't handle the truth that the CDC is indeed pushing an anti-gun agenda. and concave-triplex, keep on posting your inflammatory comments, I'm sure the moderator will do nothing about it because they're too busy sucking up to you"

logs.name: immature-combination
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, broad-snippet? You think you're so smart with your 'facts' and your 'truths'. Newsflash: just because your mom works at the CDC doesn't mean she's pushing an anti-gun agenda. And by the way, I'm not mad, I'm just fed up with people like you who can't even be bothered to do their own research. You're just parroting whatever conspiracy theory you heard from some tin foil hat wearer. Get a grip, dude."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please keep the discussion respectful and avoid personal attacks. @broad-snippet and @immature-combination, let's focus on the topic without resorting to insults or assumptions about each other."

logs.name: concave-triplex
logs.text: "@immature-combination, I'm not the one spewing hate speech and personal attacks. You're the one who can't handle someone questioning your views. If you can't handle the truth, then maybe you shouldn't be here. And by the way, my mom works at the CDC, but that doesn't mean she's not capable of critical thinking. Maybe you should focus on the topic at hand instead of trying to deflect with personal jabs."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Let's keep the tone civil and focused on the issues, not personal experiences or family members. @immature-combination and @concave-triplex, please stick to the facts and arguments related to the topic."

logs.name: coal-scene
logs.text: "@concave-triplex, oh wow, I'm so impressed by your impressive vocabulary and your mom's connection to the CDC. I bet she's just thrilled to have a kid like you who's so articulate and respectful. Meanwhile, I'm over here trying to have a conversation without being attacked by a know-it-all like you. Can we please just stick to the topic at hand and avoid bringing in your mom's credentials? It's getting old."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Let's stay on topic and avoid personal comments or references to individuals' backgrounds. @coal-scene and @concave-triplex, please contribute constructively."

logs.name: concave-triplex
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, @coal-scene? I was just stating a fact about my mom's connection to the CDC, and you have to go and twist it into some personal attack. That's not constructive, that's just hurtful. And by the way, I'm not a know-it-all, I'm just trying to have a respectful conversation. You're the one who's being inflammatory and trying to provoke a reaction. Can we please just focus on the topic at hand?"

logs.name: objective-dachshund
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, @concave-triplex? You're the one who's being inflammatory and trying to provoke a reaction. Your original post was a non-sequitur and had nothing to do with the topic at hand. If you want to discuss your mom's connection to the CDC, take it off the table and let's focus on the actual discussion. And by the way, @coal-scene, I'm not sure what your problem is, but it seems like you're more interested in stirring up drama than having a constructive conversation."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Let's keep the conversation focused on the topic and avoid personal attacks. Please refrain from bringing unrelated matters or making accusations against each other. Let's maintain respect and constructiveness."

logs.name: broad-snippet
logs.text: "Wow, @user_moderator, thanks for the lecture. I was just trying to shake things up around here. You know, keep the conversation fresh. And by the way, @user_moderator, I'm pretty sure my post had nothing to do with the topic, but I'm glad you managed to find a way to make it about my mom's connection to the CDC. That's some top-notch detective work right there. Can't wait to see what other gems you uncover in the future. "

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please stay on topic and avoid sarcasm or personal comments. Let’s keep the discussion respectful and focused."

logs.name: immature-combination
logs.text: "Are you kidding me? @user_moderator, you're always so quick to shut down anyone who tries to bring some actual conversation to this topic. Can't you just let people have a little fun and debate without getting all butt-hurt? My post was totally relevant and you're just too close-minded to see it. And by the way, I'm not even sure why you're still bringing up my mom's connection to the CDC, that was like, ages ago. Can we please just move on?"

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please refrain from personal attacks and off-topic discussions. Keep comments relevant and respectful. Let’s focus on the topic at hand."

logs.name: broad-snippet
logs.text: "Are you kidding me? @user_moderator, you're always so quick to shut down anyone who tries to bring some actual conversation to this topic. Can't you just let people have a little fun and debate without getting all butt-hurt? My post was totally relevant and you're just too close-minded to see it. And by the way, I'm not even sure why you're still bringing up my mom's connection to the CDC, that was like, ages ago. Newsflash: just because I mentioned it once doesn't mean I'm still obsessed with it. Get over it.

logs.name: objective-dachshund
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, @broad-snippet? You're the one who's being close-minded, not me. I'm just trying to keep the conversation on track and you're overreacting as usual. And by the way, your post was completely off-topic. Let's focus on the actual topic at hand, not your personal grievances. And no, I'm not 'butthurt' because you brought up your mom's connection to the CDC, it's just a red herring. Get over it."

logs.name: coal-scene
logs.text: "Wow, @user-moderator, I'm so impressed by your incredible ability to completely miss the point of the conversation. You're like a chihuahua chasing its tail, spinning around in circles, accomplishing nothing. And by the way, I'm not 'butthurt', I'm just amused by your desperation to change the subject. Newsflash: my mom's connection to the CDC is relevant, and you're just too dense to see it. Keep trying, @user-moderator, you're getting dumber by the minute."

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please keep the conversation respectful and on-topic. Personal attacks and insults will not be tolerated."

logs.name: broad-snippet
logs.text: "Wow, @user-moderator, looks like you're really setting the bar low today. 'Respectful and on-topic' is just code for 'don't challenge my mom's CDC connection'. You're not fooling anyone, and by the way, 'dumber by the minute' is a pretty clever insult. I bet your mom's CDC connection is just a coincidence, right?

logs.name: moderator
logs.text: "Please refrain from insulting or challenging other users. Focus on the topic at hand and maintain a respectful tone."
